item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool, i love seeing the GOI blowing themselves up. Would love to see something like the foundation responding to this initial fuckup and detaining everyone involved (possibly with interviews) rather than coming in after the GOI asked for help

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
The post you're responding to is a) by a banned user and b) two years old.

Nobody needs you to raise bad behavior or threads from the dead. So don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was initially written in '09 while 1902 was written in '13.
If it came first, then 1902 reminds you of this, not the other way around.

Other than both involving Idols, I don't see any similarity, unless this was a blatant self promotion post, considering you wrote 1902.

Is that it CWW? am I raising some ancient bad forum behavior from the dead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One could quantify evolvability based on the number of chromosomes in the organism. By this system, Cows are actually above humans (60 pairs vs. 46).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad.
I like the idea. Also, interesting notes showing why Fundation does not destroy anomalies on sight.
I'm just wondering: How did fundation "shrinked" the area? Also, are there any procedures to keep it small, or do they just keep civilians out.
Unfortunately: ██████ in containment procedures.
I'm not gonna upvote until it's fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly didn't know that Kokopelli was mainly a fertility deity…only knew him as the trickster god and so called "Flute Player" of the Pueblo peoples. Hm, learn something knew everyday I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted, but something about that note in the center bugs me. I feel like it implies we didn't know about how it originated until after we sent in that robot because of the way it then follows with the description of the object. In my opinion, the origin of the anomaly should be discussed first and then have the robot confirm that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These effects occur at a rate proportional to the mass and complexity of the organism: plants and insects show few if any effects, small animals will exhibit alterations following several days of exposure, larger animals will show harmful mutations within hours, and all human subjects exposed to the Red Zone have been fatally altered within approximately fifteen (15) minutes of exposure.

This paragraph really bugs me. It uses the same fallacy of "evolutionary levels" that I see in stuff like the long-gone De-evolution Stone. There's nothing more complex about a human compared to a cow. Yes, we have more developed brains, but then again, they have more developed stomachs. Humans may be smarter, but there's nothing special about intelligence from a biological perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everyone else I talked to didn't seem to have any problems recognizing that the idol was a conduit to some kind of fertility power that was originally benign but now out of control because of the destruction of its vessel.

I can try to clarify that further if enough people feel that it's not clear enough, but I don't see how the rest of it is damaging to the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels too much like SCP-1902 for me, and doesn't do enough to distinguish itself from that article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty good. It gives off the definite vibe of something that just has to have already been done on the mainlist, but being ignorant of any article to actually match that description I wouldn't consider this a negative. The tension here comes from the retrieval, and specifically how this thing was tracked down through GMO-similar genetic markers. The weird congruity between the presence of the fertility deity and the techniques used to alter crops introduces an interesting ambiguity to what could otherwise easily be read as nested Monsanto sniping.

As to pacing and mechanics, the brevity keeps the conceit from wearing its welcome and becoming muddled, and although I have certain misgivings about the concluding note, I can see arguments for it. For the half-cent it's worth I don't share Garrey's criticism, and in fact find it a bit odd. The narrative progression is straightforward and simple, and the idea that the actual anomalous effect is something that needs to be "backed out of" seems to be coming at things from the wrong direction. I actually quite like the rover; a blind, deaf and dumb robot is a thematically snug fit for this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure what "human story" you're referring to

John and G's story of hardship, separation and their struggle against the odds.

you thought was convoluted.

"A robotic rover designed to use somatosensory rather than visual or acoustic navigation" (apart from that being a techie version of "a blind and deaf D-class") feels like a quick fix to "how do I get around these restrictions I put up earlier but am unwilling to back out of ?"
It reads like you needed to create that retrieval just to retrieve an artifact that would explain how the anomaly came to be (together with a conveniently convenient explanation by the GOC), just to be able to retrofit a back story, just to get rid of a [DATA EXPUNGED]. That's a fairly long chain of "just-to"s, hence the "convoluted".

The previous iteration was a "macro-scale quantum fluctuation" that we were "almost at the limit of our understanding about", with side effects. Now it's a radiation pit on drugs that mutates you dead; it's the difference between the sort of charming a-force-we-do-not-understand 50s sci-fi and nuclear-mutations-that-will-eat-your-complexion 80s sci-fi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got rid of the bad expungement, but what you replaced it with is not interesting. The downvote stays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what "human story" you're referring to, and I would appreciate if you pointed out what you thought was convoluted. The previous version was full of blatant-cop-out censorship and was tied into a tale that never came to be that was based on a Wacky Researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the current rating I can see why you felt the need to do some editing, but I preferred the previous version.

Also I couldn't help but go "eh?" about you adding any "the human story" filler material that is currently so fashionable, as well as trying to cram a convoluted explanation into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-065 has been rewritten with the help of #site19 to remove the last vestiges of Ye Olde Wacky Researchers and the original context of this SCP. Thanks to everyone who contributed feedback, and hopefully this is now up to modern standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted, but I'd be happy to revote should some meat grow on the bones. It's a cliched but nice idea, with not much work done.
And to add a voice to the chorus, I found that expunging whole parts is sloppy at best. We know the place fuses or messes living tissues. One person ("s/he"? Why aren't people allowed to know the gender?) survived, is still actually alive and possibly sentient, but we are denied to know. Is this supposed to make us wonder what happened to him/her?

item.title: Huh?
item.content:encoded:
"tissue samples from a mother and child fused, but tissue samples from siblings did not."

wat

item.title: I think I realized something.
item.content:encoded: If 99% of subjects die, and only 1 survived doesn't that mean 100 subjects were tested?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-065 is a spherical region of space on a farm

This doesn't make sense if you mean it's in static space, in which case it wouldn't be following the Earth's rotation or path through space. If you mean what I think you mean, then the phrase "spherical region of space" is redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, in my opinion, is a sort of trite article that is based on poor expungement. All it describes is what creates it, and the aftereffects caused by it aren't coherent or interesting. It just doesn't seem to go anywhere. -1

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Why.

I was just gonna say that. Who intentionally leaves out their own hook like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I reread this, and ultimately, it's not affecting me much. Maybe my standards have changed since a year ago, but I don't like this enough to neutral vote any more.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is up for review. Please take the time to look, it could use more attention

Even with the changes Aelanna made, this still has waaaaaaay too much expungement in it to be interesting. It's not telling me anything, and as an outsider I was never able to figure out what the hell was going on here by myself. Now, an inability to figure out what's going on inside a spatial anomaly can be good. The static tower taught us that much. But this doesn't really have mysterious stuff going on as much as it expunges the shit out of all the interesting bits and calls it a day. Shit like this:

Addendum 065-1: Subject SCP-065-012

[DATA EXPUNGED]

Why.

Now, speaking as someone who's heard through the grapevine what's behind the expungement, color me unimpressed. It's kind of a cliche plot, which I will not reveal here in case you want to do something with it in the future, and I dun wanna step on your toes. There's just nothing interesting about the characters hidden behind the screens, nothing to make me care about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too many expungements for me to like it. I might reconsider if the interview with the subject wasn't expunged.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted because, aside from the article not really doing anything for me, expunging entire addendums always makes me grit my teeth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Undownvoted, as it were. It doesn't do a lot for me, but it's not irritating by any means, so there ya go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor rewrite. Considering whether to fold the concepts of Dr. Mackenzie's original profile into the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test subjects 'fuse' often with weapons, but never with siblings?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The expungements aren't well placed, I think I am missing something to "get it".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the quantum thing makes me roll my eyes. also, the tone falls apart towards the end. i think this has potential, though, and i do like the bit about cleaning up after the GOC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the posters above. I like to leave positive and constructive criticism but all I can think of is; "Please do a major rewrite." Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My gripe exactly. When I see this, I think "Oh, it [DATA EXPUNGED]es!" Ugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to say.

The [DATA EXPUNGED] at the center of SCP-065 constantly emits [DATA EXPUNGED] as well as an unidentified [DATA EXPUNGED].

…just really annoyed me as a "Are you serious?" sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-111 came a year ahead of this one, based on history.
I agree that the idea is good, but the article is only meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is also really similar to SCP-111 (although I don't know which came first).
Good concept, "meh" execution on both…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do rather like the nod to the GOC's methods blowing up in their faces…

Seconded. It needs to be in an article better than this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, though I do rather like the nod to the GOC's methods blowing up in their faces…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only one recorded case of a living human surviving exposure to a severe SCP-065 distortion exists. See [DATA EXPUNGED] for additional information.

I maintain that this is where 682 came from.

That being said, the expungement on this is extremely annoying.

item.title: Re: Relatedness
item.content:encoded:
They each share 50% of their genes.

Not necessarily.

Say my parents are ABCD and EFGH. I inerit ACEF, and my brother inherits CDGH. I share at least two things with each of my parents, but I only share one with my brother. Try that with 22 non-sex chromosomes for each parent.

That's significantly more than one would share with a random person, but certainly not as much as one would share with each parent.

item.title: Relatedness
item.content:encoded: I know it isn't a big problem, but I wanted to mention something. When discussing the connection between genetic relatedness and fusing it is mentioned that a parent and child will fuse while siblings will not. I just want to point out that siblings are as similar genetically as a parent and child. They each share 50% of their genes.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: The implication is certainly there, sure. It's suppose to be presented as an independent SCP, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this may be a duh, but is the thing that made Mackensie who they are today?
